request.question: What year was Sidwell Friends School founded where Roderick H. Cox taught for nearly 40 years?

title: Josh Hart (basketball)
Friends School of Wilmington
Tandem Friends School
J.C. Luning
Newtown Friends School
Media-Providence Friends School
Alexandria Friends School
Roderick H. Cox
Sidwell Friends School
Virginia Beach Friends School
sentences: Josh Hart (born March 6, 1995) is an American basketball player for the Los Angeles Lakers of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
He played college basketball for Villanova.
As a senior at Sidwell Friends School, Hart posted averages 24.3 points, 13.4 rebounds and 2.8 steals per game and was a highly recruited prospect.
In his sophomore season at Villanova, Hart was named the Big East Tournament most outstanding player.
As a junior, he was named to the All-Big East first team and helped Villanova win the national championship in 2016.
Friends School of Wilmington is an 18 months - 8th grade private/independent Quaker school located in Wilmington, North Carolina.
The school was founded in 1995 by the Wilmington (NC) Friends Meeting and has grown from a middle school for 30 students to a school for 190 students.
Friends School is located on two campuses located 2.9 miles apart with five buildings and eight acres of playgrounds, fields, gardens, woods, and a pond.
Tandem Friends School is a coeducational secondary school founded in 1970 in Albemarle County, Virginia, just outside Charlottesville, by educators John Howard and Duncan Alling.
In 1995, it joined the Friends Council on Education, adopting the educational beliefs and practices of the Quakers.
The current head of school is Whitney Thompson.
The Upper School, grades 9-12, has approximately 100 students, while the Middle School, grades 5-8, has approximately 120 students.
The head of the Upper School is Peter Gaines, and the head of the Middle School is Tom O'Connor.
Tandem is located on 279 Tandem Lane.
The mascot is a Quaker, though the original mascot was a badger.
When the mascot was changed in 2015, there was a good deal of resistance from students concerning the fact that though the school is technically Quaker, very few students are Quaker.
Students felt misrepresented, the more so since the badger was a mascot the students had chosen themselves, whereas the Quaker was imposed upon them.
The logo is the historic main building (originally a plantation house and later a Civil War hospital), which is thought by some to be haunted.
John Christian Luning (December 21, 1863 – September 28, 1928) was a mayor of the town of Leesburg, Florida, during the 1890s and Lake County Commissioner.
He would go on to serve the shortest term as Florida's Commissioner of Agriculture for 14 days before going on to serve as State Treasurer of Florida.

He was part of a group of Florida leaders known as the "Three Musketeers" – along with James B. Whitfield (a state treasurer and state attorney general before serving nearly 40 years on the Florida Supreme Court) and William V. Knott (who held various cabinet positions for nearly 40 continuous years before becoming a political appointee) – and had a bright political future before his sudden death in September 1928.
Newtown Friends School is a coeducational prekindergarten through eighth grade Quaker independent school founded in 1948 and located in Bucks County, Pennsylvania.
Though its name and address indicate it is in Newtown Township, it is physically located in Middletown Township.
Newtown Friends' campus adjoins with that of George School.
Media-Providence Friends School is a Quaker school founded as Media Friends School in Media, Pennsylvania in 1876.
Alexandria Friends School (AFS) was a private, non-profit Friends (Quaker) middle and high school (grades 6-12) located at 3830 Seminary Road in Alexandria, Virginia, United States.
It was founded in 2006 by faculty and staff from Thornton Friends School's Alexandria campus, which closed at the end of the 2005-2006 school year.
The school closed in 2012, despite promises to reopen the following year.
Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete, football player, teacher, and environmentalist.
He was the NCAA champion in the hammer throw in 1933 and also played for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1930 to 1932.
Cox later served in the United States Navy and attained the rank of commander in the Naval Reserve.
He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.
Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., offering pre-kindergarten through secondary school classes.
Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell, its motto is ""Eluceat omnibus lux" " (English: Let the light shine out from all ), alluding to the Quaker concept of inner light.
All Sidwell Friends students attend Quaker meeting for worship weekly, and middle school students begin every day with five minutes of silence.
Virginia Beach Friends School (VBFS) is an independent life-skills and college preparatory day school founded in 1955 under the care of the Virginia Beach Friends Meeting.

Virginia Beach Friends School has more than 200 students enrolled in four divisions – Early School (Cottage, Treehouse, Pre-K and Kindergarten), Lower School (Grades 1-5), Middle School (Grades 6-8), and Upper School (Grades 9-12).

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What year was Sidwell Friends School founded where Roderick H. Cox taught for nearly 40 years?
hop1_docs: Roderick H. Cox | Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete, football player, teacher, and environmentalist.  He was the NCAA champion in the hammer throw in 1933 and also played for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1930 to 1932.  Cox later served in the United States Navy and attained the rank of commander in the Naval Reserve.  He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.

Sidwell Friends School | Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., offering pre-kindergarten through secondary school classes.  Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell, its motto is ""Eluceat omnibus lux" " (English: Let the light shine out from all ), alluding to the Quaker concept of inner light.  All Sidwell Friends students attend Quaker meeting for worship weekly, and middle school students begin every day with five minutes of silence.

Roderick Cox (conductor) | Roderick Cox is an African American conductor.  He is currently associate conductor of the Minnesota Orchestra, a position he was appointed to in September 2016 after serving as assistant conductor for a year.

Marvin Chirelstein | Marvin A. Chirelstein (October 8, 1928 - February 16, 2015) was a Professor Emeritus of Law at Columbia Law School, where he taught for nearly 30 years.  He taught a contracts course to first year law students as well as tax and corporate finance courses.  Prior to teaching at Columbia, Professor Chirelstein taught for over 15 years at Yale Law School.  He authored several course books on law.

Joe Cox (American football) | Cox started at Independence High School in Charlotte, North Carolina.  Cox was named to the 2004 "Parade Magazine" All-America Team and "Super Prep" All-Mid Atlantic Team.  He was the North Carolina Gatorade Player of the Year.  Cox was on the "AP" first-team All-State team and was a two-time "Charlotte Observer" Offensive Player of the Year.  He also played in the Shrine Bowl of the Carolinas and impressed scouts by going 31-0 as a starting quarterback.  Cox was a two-time team captain and was the number 7 rated quarterback in the country by "Rivals.com" and Tom Lemming of ESPN.  He set a North Carolina record with 66 touchdown passes while taking his team to its fifth consecutive state championship his senior year.  Cox was named the MVP of the state title game twice.  As a junior, Cox threw for nearly 4,000 yards and over 40 touchdowns.  As a senior, he completed 240 out of 363 passes for over 4,500 yards and only five interceptions.

Roderick Sprague | Roderick Sprague III (February 18, 1933 – August 20, 2012) was a renowned American anthropologist, ethnohistorian and historical archaeologist, and the Emeritus Director of the Laboratory of Anthropology at the University of Idaho in Moscow, where he taught for thirty years.  He had extensive experience in environmental impact research, trade beads, aboriginal burial customs, and the Columbia Basin area.

Priscilla Baird | Priscilla Baird (1828-1904) was a pioneering teacher in Missouri and Illinois and an advocate for girls' education.  She began her career in Shelbyville, Missouri in 1851.  She then taught at Liberty Female College and Lancaster schools before relocating to Illinois during the civil war and teaching at the Springfield High School.  Returning to Missouri, she taught at Ingleside College in Palmyra, Missouri and at Hardin College in Mexico, Missouri.  After completing nearly thirty years of teaching, she founded the Baird College in 1885, where she remained until her retirement in 1897.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Roderick H. Cox: passage 1 ("Roderick H. Cox") — quote: "a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years." (passage 1).
- Sidwell Friends School: passage 2 ("Sidwell Friends School") — quote: "Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell." (passage 2).

2) Ambiguities:
- Name variants/candidates present: "Roderick H. Cox | Roderick Howard Cox" (passage 1) and a separate "Roderick Cox (conductor)" (passage 3). These are distinct candidate pages; do not assume which is intended beyond passage 1's match.
- No alternate Sidwell Friends School pages/variants appear in the provided passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as in the passages): "Sidwell Friends School".
- Precise fact needed to answer the question: the school's founding year. That founding year appears already in passage 2 ("Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell"), so no additional factual element appears missing from the provided passages.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "Sidwell Friends School" page — check the lede/infobox and the "History" or "Founding" section for the founding year and founder line (verify "Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell").
  Example search: "Sidwell Friends School founded 1883 lede/infobox".
- Open "Roderick H. Cox" page — check the lede/infobox and "Career"/teaching sections to confirm the Sidwell connection and "nearly 40 years" wording.
  Example search: "Roderick H. Cox Sidwell teacher nearly 40 years lede".
- If needed for name disambiguation, open "Roderick Cox (conductor)" or a "Roderick Cox" disambiguation page to confirm distinct persons and avoid misattribution.

Final note: I am deferring the final answer until those pages/sections are fetched and verified.
hop2_docs: Sidwell Friends School | Sidwell Friends School is a highly selective Quaker school located in Bethesda, Maryland and Washington, D.C., offering pre-kindergarten through secondary school classes.  Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell, its motto is ""Eluceat omnibus lux" " (English: Let the light shine out from all ), alluding to the Quaker concept of inner light.  All Sidwell Friends students attend Quaker meeting for worship weekly, and middle school students begin every day with five minutes of silence.

Roderick H. Cox | Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete, football player, teacher, and environmentalist.  He was the NCAA champion in the hammer throw in 1933 and also played for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1930 to 1932.  Cox later served in the United States Navy and attained the rank of commander in the Naval Reserve.  He was a teacher at the Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.

Alexandria Friends School | Alexandria Friends School (AFS) was a private, non-profit Friends (Quaker) middle and high school (grades 6-12) located at 3830 Seminary Road in Alexandria, Virginia, United States.  It was founded in 2006 by faculty and staff from Thornton Friends School's Alexandria campus, which closed at the end of the 2005-2006 school year.  The school closed in 2012, despite promises to reopen the following year.

Steve Sidwell (American football) | Steve Sidwell (born August 30, 1944) is a former American football coach.  He coached in the National Football League (NFL) for 22 years and was a college football coach for 16 years.

Wellsprings Friends School | Wellsprings Friends School is a private non-profit alternative high school in Eugene, Oregon, United States.  It was founded in 1994 by members of the Eugene Friends Meeting, a local Quaker church.  It serves a maximum of 65 students each year in grades 9-12.

Wilmington Friends School | Wilmington Friends School is a preschool-12th grade, Quaker school, in Wilmington, Delaware.  Today, the school is just above 7% Quaker.  At Friends, global education encompasses signature international programs (International Baccalaureate, inter-divisional Chinese, School Year Abroad) and an overall approach that teaches students to engage complex issues intellectually and in action, in and outside of the classroom, with a sense of confidence and responsibility.  The school was founded in 1748 by members of the Wilmington Monthly Meeting of Friends (Quakers).

School on Magnolia | The School on Magnolia was an alternative high school in Cleveland, Ohio.  It was founded in 1970 as the Friends School in Cleveland, and its association with the Religious Society of Friends continued through 1975, at which point the local Friends meeting decided that the school was no longer fully carrying out the mission of a Quaker school.  The change was amicable, and the school was renamed The School on Magnolia, after its location in a large old house at 10819 Magnolia Drive in the University Circle section of Cleveland.  The school initially served a wide range of students who had found traditional high schools did not meet their needs, but in its later years it increasingly functioned as more of a social service institution for troubled teens.  It eventually moved to a new location nearer downtown, and was renamed the Eleanor Gerson School, after a woman who had for many years served on its board of trustees.
response.summary_2: Founded in 1883 by Thomas Sidwell. Roderick Howard Cox (March 5, 1911 – August 25, 2000) was an American track and field athlete and teacher who taught at Sidwell Friends School for nearly 40 years.
